title: IICRC S500:2021 Overview — The Current Standard for Professional Water Damage Restoration
description: A plain-English overview of ANSI/IICRC S500:2021 (5th edition) — the current Standard for Professional Water Damage Restoration — and how Australian restoration technicians apply its category, class, drying, and documentation framework. Referenced by RestoreAssist throughout.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## IICRC S500: The Foundational Standard

The IICRC S500 Standard for Professional Water Damage Restoration is the primary technical reference for the water damage restoration industry globally, including Australia. The current edition is ANSI/IICRC S500:2021 (the 5th edition, published in 2021). It defines the categories and classes of water damage, methodologies for assessment, drying, and documentation, and the professional responsibilities of the restorer.

When you write a scope or a report, you should cite the current edition — S500:2021 — and reference a specific section only where you are quoting or applying that section directly. RestoreAssist cites S500:2021 throughout.

## What the Standard Covers

### Category of Water

S500 classifies water by the level of contamination at the source, which drives the health precautions and the restore-versus-remove decision:

- Category 1 — water from a sanitary source (for example, a supply line or melted ice) that poses no substantial risk from ingestion or contact.
- Category 2 — significantly contaminated water ("grey water") with the potential to cause discomfort or sickness, such as discharge from a dishwasher or washing machine.
- Category 3 — grossly contaminated water ("black water") that may contain pathogenic, toxigenic, or otherwise harmful agents, such as sewage or rising ground and surface water.

Category can deteriorate over time and with contact — clean water left in contact with soiled materials, or standing for an extended period, may need to be reclassified upward.

### Class of Water Intrusion

Class describes the evaporation load — how much water the affected materials have absorbed and how difficult the structure will be to dry. It ranges from Class 1 (the least amount of water and absorption) through to Class 4 (specialty drying situations involving low-porosity or deeply saturated materials such as hardwood, plaster, brick, and concrete). Class informs the drying strategy and the equipment load.

### Inspection, Drying, and Documentation

S500 sets out how to inspect and make a preliminary determination, how to establish and verify drying goals, and how to document the job. Good practice — reflected in the standard — is to record psychrometric conditions (temperature, relative humidity, and specific humidity) alongside material moisture readings, to establish the drying goal at the start of the job, and to confirm that goal has been achieved before demobilising equipment.

## What This Means for Australian Practitioners

Australian insurers and assessors expect restoration reports to reference the current edition of S500 — S500:2021 — and to justify the category and class determinations that drive the scope. Documentation templates, scope libraries, and training materials should all cite S500:2021.

In RestoreAssist, scope templates, drying-goal validation, and equipment calculations are aligned to S500:2021, so if you are working through the platform you are citing the current standard.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. In this overview I want to give you a clear picture of the IICRC S500 standard — the foundational standard for professional water damage restoration — and how you apply it in the field.

First, the edition. The current standard is ANSI/IICRC S500:2021, the 5th edition, published in 2021. When you write a scope or a report, cite S500:2021, and reference a specific section only where you are directly applying it. Don't cite a section number you can't stand behind.

Let me run through what the standard actually gives you.

Category of water. S500 classifies water by how contaminated it is at the source. Category 1 is clean water from a sanitary source, like a supply line. Category 2 is grey water — significantly contaminated, such as a dishwasher or washing-machine discharge. Category 3 is black water — grossly contaminated, such as sewage or rising ground water. And remember category can deteriorate: clean water left in contact with soiled materials, or standing too long, may need to be reclassified upward.

Class of water intrusion. Class is about the evaporation load — how much water the materials have absorbed and how hard the structure will be to dry. Class 1 is the least; Class 4 is specialty drying, where you're dealing with low-porosity or deeply saturated materials like hardwood, plaster, brick, and concrete. Your class drives your drying strategy and how much equipment you need.

Inspection, drying, and documentation. S500 sets out how to inspect and make your preliminary determination, how to establish and verify your drying goals, and how to document the job. Record your psychrometric conditions — temperature, relative humidity, and specific humidity — alongside material moisture readings. Set your drying goal in writing at the start, and confirm you've hit it before you pull equipment.

Why does this matter in Australia? Insurers and assessors expect your reports to reference the current edition — S500:2021 — and to justify the category and class that drive your scope.

In RestoreAssist, scope templates, drying-goal validation, and equipment calculations are all aligned to S500:2021, so you're citing the current standard by default.

Head to restoreassist.app to learn more.
